summary: DeVault introduces the Gemini protocol as a simple, encrypted alternative to the web for exchanging hypertext documents. He argues that web browsers have become impossibly complex and that the web's problems are fundamentally unsolvable within its current framework. Rather than trying to create a 'sane subset' of web standards, Gemini offers a completely different medium that addresses a subset of the web's use cases far more effectively. He highlights Gemini's deliberate simplicity, resistance to extensibility, and strict separation of content from presentation as key virtues, and showcases several Gemini tools he has built in C and Go.
key_points: Gemini is a simple, TLS-encrypted protocol for hypertext exchange that uses its own document format (gemtext) and can be implemented by a single person in an afternoon
Web browsers are irredeemably complex, and building a new one that implements sufficient web standards would produce an unmaintainable, insecure monstrosity
Gemini's deliberate resistance to extensibility is a feature, not a bug — extensibility is what led the web to its current bloated state
Gemini strictly separates content (server's role) from presentation (client's role), making clients true user agents rather than vendor agents
The answer is not 'the web but less of it' — there is no sane subset of web standards, so a fundamentally different medium is needed
money_quotes: Successfully completing that implementation, if even possible, would necessarily produce a Lovecraftian mess: unmaintainable, full of security vulnerabilities, with gigabytes in RAM use and hours in compile times.
All of the contemporary web browsers that implement a sufficiently useful subset of web standards are ass and getting assier.
The problem is unsolvable. We cannot have the 'web' without all of these problems.
A client or server implementation can be written from scratch by a single person in the space of an afternoon or two. A new web browser could take hundreds of engineers millions of hours to complete.
Extensibility is generally a bad idea. Extensions ultimately lead to more complexity and Gemini might suffer the same fate as the web if not for its disdain for extensions.
It allows clients to be simpler and act more as user agents than vendor agents.
I don't think there is a 'sane' subset of those standards.
key_insight: The web's complexity is an unsolvable problem inherent to its design, so rather than trying to fix it, we should embrace fundamentally simpler alternatives like Gemini that deliberately resist the extensibility that doomed the web.
